titleStmt.title: Saudi Arabia 2022 – Speaker 14 – Education (Girls) (Part 2)
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Saudi Arabia Fieldwork Campaign Gunda Kinzl 2022

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: So before they used to teach only the boys, or how was it?
Those who could afford it would educate their sons, if he could afford it he would educate them.
But with regards to the daughter, they did not invest (lit. protect) in her much, you know? All of them, not just the Rašāyda, all of them.
In the same schools we had older women
my sisters even noticed that when I got in (school), I was there for seven years teaching older women
They noticed that as I was teaching them and staying with the older people, I liked spending time with them
I tell you, to inherite happiness you should spend time with the youngsters and with the older people.

They told me, indeed Ḥanān you have changed, I noticed that your manner of speech has changed, here we don't say dialect, your words have changed, and this was because we were living amongst them, we lived with them, with the older women and their dialect
and additionally there were people who were protective of their dialect, it wasn't like that of my mother or ... there were older women whose dialect was the original one.
??? We liked to sit with them and they would come ... they liked to sit with us, when we didn't have classes, and tell us stories.
There were older women and young girls, from the ages of fifteen, twelve, they couldn't enroll in the morning (regular) school
They could only go to the adult education (school) in the afternoon, at three o'clock the school would start, and we would finish at six o'clock.
And indeed, I lived with them for seven years, and after that I started working in an official position
What was it?
It was ... and most of them you see, a lot of Rašīdi female teachers worked in it (this kind of job) here
Even if the salary was low, the amount we used to get was low, but it was offered, it was there, there weren't any official jobs with a high salary, there weren't any.
And God willing, even when I left them, they were striving to learn, you could see this persistence in them, they were striving to learn at an advanced age
Even in areas, you went to al-Ləḥən, (in areas like al-Ləḥən which you have seen), the houses are small and few in these areas
they used to go and submit their papers for (the introduction of) education, they would say we want a school for educating older women, this thing was ...
Nice, excellent.
Yes, and they would offer a house, I remember when we worked in one house, he (the owner) was living in Kuwait.
they had asked him if he could offer (open) ...
the house which originally was empty
yes, the empty (house), in the part which was attached (part of the house which was attached to the main house), like so, they said we want to study in it.
And indeed they proceeded with it and the school was established, I worked there possibly for half a year
and finally that school was actually two schools, and after working at that school I left and I was posted at a morning school officially, I was an official teacher.

Do you know? They strove (for these schools) and the people who worked and established these schools, they went, meaning a graduated woman would say I would like to open a school for the education of older women, and so they would tell her 'go around the area and the village which you want (to open the school in) and bring me the names of those who would like to study' and if they managed to accumulate a certain number they would say 'ok let's go ahead and open a school.'
Yes, one of our neighbours started off like this, she went to al-Ləfət and the people signed 'we want (a school)' and so they opened a school for her, it was in Zabarān
Very good for her
Yes, they were all ... this was in the beginning, you see? The schools for the erasure of illiteracy, they did a lot for the area.
This is to the extent that it preceded the computer, the computer now does everything, meaning now you log on and you graduate, and that's it

If you took the official papers, we used to write the levels on them like so, there wasn't anything like the computer or something that would log an official record in the system, and things like this.
They used to study for three years, the school (scholastic program) was three years long, they graduated, and they used to re-enroll for a second time, if a woman felt like she didn't learn.
But when the computer (came) it stopped allowing her to do this, because she was registered as a graduate, that's it you have graduated already

Yes, a lot of people have forgotten (about these things). And the state would pay a bonus in the beginning, for the education of the older women, the woman who was interested in education would earn a bonus
Yes, I wasn't ... I didn't experience this, it was before, before my time.
There was a bonus, and they additionally strove to enroll because additionally, because of the bonus, meaning it provided incentives for them: the social security and the bonus

Do you see? But it works, it worked on the younger girls ... I left a couple of years ago, yes, I left them, how long ago was that? I left them approximately fourteen years ago, the schools, the proper school, the official school
two or three years I came across a woman in the pharmacy, she was one of my students
God bless, the girl is now older ### I didn't recognize her but she recognized me
I was there with ... (my son)
He had to be vaccinated, the needle, and she recognized me and said are you (full name)?
Even with the cover?
I think (my face) was revealed
I see
Yes, she said are you (full name)?
I said yes, she said ... because she got older and her look changed, yes

I said who are you? She said I am so and so, I remembered the name, because she was an excellent student, they were Bedouins, they were Bedouins, and she and her sisters were three in number, and they were excellent (or she was excellent)
Mean God bless it worked ... the occasion was indeed nice, I said 'where did you go, how have you been, what have you been doing?' and she said 'I am at the faculty, I graduated.'

I didn't know that she had graduated, but they had completed their education, and after that she also graduated from the three years of primary education, which is the (program of) the school for the erasure of illiteracy, and that's it, she went to middle school, they completed it.
Thank God, this is nice
Yes, their efforts ...
And this was after you?
What do you mean?
Meaning because of you
Because of all of us, you see?
very well
yes, the younger ones went on to finish but the older women no, but at least it was good that they could read and write.
Of course
yes
And I also felt that if the mother was educated, then it was only natural that her daughters were educated in their childhood.
Yes, yes, they would say 'we don't know how to teach our young children', yes, and additionally they didn't know how to read things and write
They needed this, they were young, they were young meaning they were in their thirties, their fourties, they would need to know how to read
And there were some people, they were old, slightly old, but here the area served them (worked for them), and the women here could learn
God's blessings on Muḥammad
I will remember some other topics to you
That's it let us ... this is the point that I wanted to tell you about, that it was achieved, female education was achieved and this was its beginning, this was how they all began

And the majority now, no, the majority of the graduates in that area all of them are unemployed, unfortunately, they got their degrees and they couldn't find a position, there aren't any jobs, there aren't any jobs, there's no demand
The important (job) is nursing, nursing, it started, by God how will I tell you? I am not able to pinpoint a specific year (?????)
